coredata.dc:title: Stable static zinc-iodine redox battery constructed with graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yizhi
Chen, Nan
Wang, Zhe
Xu, Shaohui
Xiong, Dayuan
Wang, Lianwei
Yang, Pingxiong
Chu, Paul K.
coredata.dc:description: A highly stable static zinc-iodine redox battery is constructed using graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt which improves stripping of the plated zinc and decreases complexation between zinc and iodine ions due to the smaller oxidizing consistuent on the graphite felt surface. The static zinc-iodine redox battery can be operated at high currents while delivering excellent performance with regard to the discharging capacity, energy, as well as cycling stability. There is a promising commerical prospect in the field of power batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc-iodine redox battery
Complexation
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt (GQD-GF)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Static Zn–I redox batteries consisting of graphite felt (GF) and graphene quantum dots recoated graphite felt (GQD-GF) electrodes: Voltage-time curves for the first three periods for concentrations from 1 to 5 M at charging/discharging currents of (a) 100 mA and (b) 200 mA and (c) Voltage drops; (d) Discharge capacity versus cycle number for the static Zn–I redox battery in 3.0 M (square) and 5 M (circle) ZnI2 solutions at a current of 200 mA.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms (CV) of graphite felt in ZnI2 solution with concentrations of 1 M (II), 2 M (III), 3 M (IV), and 5 M (V). The CV curve of the graphite stick in the 3 M ZnI2 solution is also shown in (I). The scanning rate is 100 mV-s−1. Successive cyclic voltammograms of the graphite felt in (b) 1 M, (c) 2 M, (d) 3 M, and (e) 5 M ZnI2 solutions at an anodic potential of −0.3 V and limited cathodic potential from −0.7 to 1.0 V. The scanning rate is 10 mV-s−1.

Cyclic voltammograms of the graphite felt in the 3 M ZnI2 solutions at an anodic potential of −0.3 V and cathodic potential limit from (a) −0.7, (b) −0.75, (c) −0.8, and (d) −0.85 V. The scanning rates are varied from 10 to 100 mV-s−1.

Cyclic voltammograms of the graphite felt in (a) 1 M, (b) 2 M, (c) 3 M, and (d) 5 M ZnI2 solutions in the potential region from −0.3 to −1.05 V. The scanning rates are varied from 10 to 100 mV-s−1; (e) Oxidation peak area versus Zn–I concentrations for different sweeping rates.
EDS spectra of the graphite felt deposited with zinc in the 3 M ZnI2 solution at (a) Low and (b) High potentials. The deposited time is 30 min and the insets show the SEM images and elemental ratios.

Successive cyclic voltammograms of the graphene quantum dots recoated graphite felt (GQD-GF) in (a) 3 M and (b) 5 M ZnI2 solutions. The scanning rate is 10 mV-s−1. Cyclic voltammograms of the graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt (GQD-GF) in the 3 M ZnI2 solution for an anodic potential of −0.1 V and cathodic potential limit change from (c) −0.65, (d) −0.7, (e) −0.75, and (f) −0.85 V. The scanning rates are varied from 10 to 100 mV-s−1.

Properties of the static Zn–I redox battery (SZIRB) operated at different currents as a function of Zn–I concentrations (1.0–5.0 M) (Charge capacity 310 mAh). Graphite felt and graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt (GQD-GF) electrodes are selected to fabricate SZIRBs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: I: operation current (mA) and current density (mA.cm−1); C: ZnI2 concentration (mol L−1); Q: Discharge capacities (mAh); E: Discharge energy (mWh); CE (VE, EE): Coulombic (Voltage, Energy) efficiency (%); V1: Charging voltage (V); V2: Discharging voltage (V); ΔV: voltage drop (V).

head.ce:title: Stable static zinc-iodine redox battery constructed with graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices
And School of Physics and Electronic Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly stable static zinc-iodine redox battery is constructed using graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt which improves stripping of the plated zinc and decreases complexation between zinc and iodine ions due to the smaller oxidizing consistuent on the graphite felt surface. The static zinc-iodine redox battery can be operated at high currents while delivering excellent performance with regard to the discharging capacity, energy, as well as cycling stability. There is a promising commerical prospect in the field of power batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the large volumetric energy capacity (5.85 Ahcm−3), abundant reserve in the earth crust (24th most abundant element, 0.0075 wt%), non-flammability, and excellent electrochemical properties in aqueous media, aqueous rechargeable zinc-based batteries have received extensive attention [1–7]. In particular, hybrid zinc-based aqueous flow batteries such as zinc-ployiodide flow batteries (ZIFBs) [8–13] are interesting because of the high theoretical energy density of 322 Wh-L−1 at the solubility limit of ZnI2 (∼7 mol-L−1). Experimentally, the maximum discharge energy density is 167 Wh-L−1 in a nearly neutral electrolyte containing 5.0 M ZnI2 due to the highly soluble iodide/triiodide redox couple. The Zn–I redox flow systems separate energy storage and power generation by storing the electroactive species in externally flowing electrolytes (anolyte and catholyte). In the cathode stack, the redox electrolyte capitalizes on virtues such as fast ion physisorption confined in carbon nanopores and/or electric double layer formation corresponding to the conversion reaction between iodide and triiodide ions, whereas the redox reactions at the anode occur inside another stack corresponding to Zn plating/stripping. The iodide ion redox process has attracted much interest because the theoretical capacity of the Zn–I flow battery is governed by when all of the iodide ions are fully converted into triiodide ions at the cathode half-cell [8,9]. The suitable additive complexing agent such as Br-ions can be used to stabilize the free iodine and increase the battery capacity. Hence, a zinc/iodine–bromide battery can be designed to achieve a larger energy density by exploiting bromide ions as a complexing agent to free up one-third of the iodide ions that act as a complexing agent to stabilize iodine (I2) to form triiodide, so that the useable capacity of iodide can be increased without forming irreversible free iodine [10].

Depict the impressive energy density, the Zn–I redox flow batteries have only been adopted for large-scale energy storage in grid-level applications due to the large space required by the pumping devices and reservoirs. Therefore, reducing the pumps and tanks can shrink the required space to spur application to smaller power batteries. The static Zn–I redox batteries have recently been proposed [14–17]. They are based on encapsulated iodine in microporous carbon to form the cathode materials [15] and a double layer cathode configuration with a conductor layer and adsorptive layer [16]. In fact, the metal–organic framework (MOF) [17] has been exploited as the ionic sieve membrane to simultaneously manipulate iodine cathode species to suppress shuttling of triiodide ions to the zinc anode and mitigate parasitic side reactions on the Zn surface. However, in static Zn–I redox batteries, metallic zinc typically serves as the anodic electrode in the aqueous electrolyte. In this respect, the hybrid non-flow redox electrolyte-aided energy storage systems have aroused interest [18] and the zinc–iodine single flow battery (ZISFB) has been proposed [19], but it is challenging to produce the static Zn–I redox system based on the carbon electrode in the Zn–I aqueous electrolyte. The zinc–iodine single flow battery (ZISFB) has been fabricated by selecting a porous positive electrode sealed in the static electrolyte without a pipeline and pump on the cathode electrode side. It can also suppress the side reaction of O2 oxidation and inhibit water transfer. Even in the presence of only one pump on the anode side (Zn plating/stripping), the zinc–iodine single flow battery (ZISFB) shows high Coulombic efficiency (∼97%) and energy efficiency (∼80%) at a current density of 40 mA cm−2, and the battery can run continuously for more than 500 cycles [19]. However the cycling stability still poses a challenge for static Zn–I redox batteries composed of both carbon electrodes because the zinc halide complexes (Zn-X-, X = Br or I) are formed in the high concentration solutions [20–22], which can influence the performance of the battery. In the electrochemical process, zinc ions are the active redox species and charge carriers [8] and highly reversible zinc plating/stripping processes are important [1,2,4,23–25], especially for the static Zn–I redox batteries. Moreover, there is insufficient understanding of the complicated reactions involving iodine species when plating Zn on the porous carbon electrode in an aqueous electrolyte with a high concentration. In this work, a static zinc-iodine redox battery (SZIRB) is designed and fabricated. The results show that the properties of the static Zn–I redox battery can be improved by adding surface functional groups to graphite felt such as graphene quantum dots to control oxidation and complexation of iodine and zinc-iodide.

Citric acid, acetone, and NaOH were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. and all the chemicals reagents were analytical grade and used without purification. The 5.0 mm thick graphite felt (GF)served as the working electrode in the experiments. The GF was sonicated in acetone for 30 min and deionized water (18 MΩ) for 30 min and then dried for 6 h at 80° on a hot plate. The graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt (GQD-GF) were fabricated by two steps. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were first synthesized by a ‘‘bottom–up’’ technique [26,27]. For an organic precursor, Citric acid was selected to prepare GQDs by tuning the suitable carbonization degree. Typically, citric acid was put in a beaker and heated to 200 °C for about 30 min until citric acid changed into an orange liquid which indicate the formation of GQD. 20 g-L−1 NaOH was added dropwise to obtain a pH of 7 to form the aqueous solution of GQDs. The graphite felt was placed in 25 mL of the GQD solution together with 25 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 60 min. The graphene quantum dots coated graphite felts (GQD-GF) were washed with deionized water ultrasonically for 30 min and dried for 6 h.

The schematic of the static Zn–I redox battery is depicted in Supplementary Fig. S1a (left). Two treated graphite plates were the current collectors and (GQD-coated) graphite felt was the negative electrode (area of 15 cm2). The working and counter electrodes were separated by a membrane (Nafion 115). In order to restrict the influence of zinc dendrites, a glassy fiber was used in the negative electrode. The electrolyte was injected by vacuum back filling. The volume of the injected solution was about 21 mL for the positive cell and 7 mL for the negative cell. The image of the static Zn–I redox battery was shown in Supplementary Fig. S1a (right). The galvanostatic charging–discharging tests were performed under different conditions to investigate the reversibility based on the Land system (Wuhan Land, China). For simplification, the charge capacity was 310 mAh.

The sample morphology and composition were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Electrochemical experiments were carried out on a three-electrode electrochemical working station (Bio-logic, VMP3) in 1–5 M (where M is molarity, mol-L−1) aqueous ZnI2solutions with the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum electrode being the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed in the potential range between −1.2 and −0.6 V versus SCE at scanning rates from 10 to 100 mV-s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed in the frequency range from 0.01 to 10,000 Hz at the OCP potential. All the measurements were performed at room temperature (25 °C) and the results were normalized by the footprint area.

In order to study the stability of the static Zn–I redox battery (SZIRB), three batteries filled with the 5.0 M Zn–I electrolyte are fabricated and operated at different currents of 100 mA, 200 mA, and 300 mA for the same capacity limit of 310 mAh. The charging-discharging curves in Supplementary Fig. 1b show the stability for the first 5 cycles. When the current goes up, the charging (discharging) voltage increases (decreases) and the capacity increases (∼250 mAh). The characteristics of the SZIRB are summarized in Supplementary Table S1, which shows that the capacity increases from 248.3 to 276.5 mAh as the current goes up from 100 to 300 mA and the Coulombic efficiency (CE) increases from 80.1 to 89.2%. However the energy efficacy decreases from 71.2 to 67.7% due to the decreasing voltage efficiency from 89.1 to 77.7%. At the same time, the charging voltage increases from 1.246 to 1.358 V and the discharging voltage decreases from 1.11 to 1.055 V, while the discharge energy is almost the same (271–274 mWh) for currents between 100 and 300 mA. The experimental results indicate the repeatability of the fabrication process of the SZIRBs. In addition, the discharge polarization curves and rate performance including of Coulombic efficiency, voltage efficiency and energy efficiency are shown in Supplementary Fig. S2. Then the SZIRBs are operated at 100 and 200 mA to obtained high efficiencies and operation voltages.

In our experiments, static Zn–I redox batteries (SZIRB) with different Zn–I concentrations (1–5 M) are produced. The galvanostatic charging-discharging curves of the 7 initial periods are shown in Fig. 1 for currents of 100 mA (a) and 200 mA (b). At a low current (100 mA, Fig. 1a), a high discharging potential (1.2 V) is observed for a low concentration (1.0 M) in addition to a high charging voltage (1.32 V), low capacity (170 mAh), and cycling stability. The capacity decreases to 150 mAh in the 7 cycles. For higher concentrations (3.0–5.0 M), the capacity reaches 250 mAh and is stable. At a larger current (200 mA), Fig. 1b shows that the capacity decreases from 200 mAh to 150 mAh for a low concentration (1.0 M). In comparison, for higher concentrations (3.0–5.0 M), the capacity reaches 260 mAh and increases in the 7 cycles.

The properties of the SZIRBs including the discharge capacities (energies) and Coulombic (voltage, energy) efficiencies are presented in Table 1 . At a low current (100 mA), the capacity increases from 173.1 (1.0 M) to 256.1 (3.0 M) mAh with increasing electrolyte concentrations and decreases to 248.4 mAh for a higher concentration (5.0 M). In addition, the discharge energy increases from 208.0 (1.0 M) to 284.0 mWh (3.0 M) and decreases to 272.0 mWh (5.0 M). At the same time, the Coulombic efficiency (CE) increases from 55.8 (1.0 M) to 82.6 (3.0 M) and decreases to 80.1% (5.0 M). For the low current case, the drop with a high electrolyte concentration (5.0 M) can be attributed to the larger electrolyte resistance arising from the electrolyte concentration [8]. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) in Supplementary Fig. S4 reveals the cell resistance from the x-intercept in the Nyquist plots, which show that the solution resistances increases with Zn–I concentration. It increases from 6.3 to 6.8 Ω as the concentration is increased from 1.0 to 2.0 M. The resistance reaches about 75 Ω for 5.0 M Zn–I solution compared to 3.1 Ω for 3.0 M Zn–I. Due to the large resistance for 5.0 M Zn–I solution, the energy efficiency decrease from 74.7 (3 M) to 71.2% (5 M) even the voltage efficiency increase from 87.3 to 89.1%.

At a high current (200 mA), the capacity increases from 208.0 to 255.1 and 264.0 mAh when the electrolyte concentration is raised from 1.0 to 3.0 and 5.0 M and the discharge energy increases from 249.0 to 254.0 and 280.0 mWh. At the same time, the Coulombic efficiency (CE) increases from 67.1 to 82.3 and to 85.2%. Therefore, the battery properties improve with increasing electrolyte concentrations from 1.0 to 5.0 M at a high current 200 mA besides the discharge capacity (energy) and Coulombic efficiency. However, for the 3 M electrolyte, it shows the lowest voltage efficiency (74.4%) even almost the same discharge capacity and Coulombic efficiency are observed for increasing current and the discharge energy decreases from 284 (100 mA) to 254 mWh (200 mA) with increasing current.

The average charging/discharging voltages and voltage drops are summarized in Table 1. The charging voltage decreases from 1.30 to 1.26 (1.23) V as the concentration of Zn–I goes up from 1.0 to 3.0 (5.0) M at a current 100 mA. However, the discharge voltage shows the smallest value (1.10 V) for the 3.0 M Zn–I electrolyte, that is less than those for 1.0 M (1.20 V) and even 5.0 M (1.14 V). The largest voltage drop (0.16 V) is observed for 3.0 M compared to 1.0 M (0.10 V) and 5.0 M (0.09). At a current of 200 mA, the charging voltage is 1.35 V and insensitive to the solution concentration. The discharging voltage shows the smallest value for 3 M (0.99 V) compared to 1.0 M (1.19 V) and 5.0 M (1.02 V). The larger voltage drop (0.34 V) is observed for 3.0 M comparing to 1.0 M (0.15 V) and the same voltage drop appears for 5.0 M despite different solution resistance. Fig. 1c shows the average charging/discharging voltages as a function of Zn–I concentrations and the large voltage drop arises from the small discharge voltage in the 3.0 M Zn–I electrolyte. Owing to the large voltage drop in the 3.0 M Zn–I electrolyte, the battery capacity decreases from ∼250 mAh to ∼100 mAh over 50 cycles at a current of 200 mA, as shown in Fig. 1d. For the static Zn–I redox battery (concentration 3.0 M), the charge-discharge plot shows the stability charging/discharging voltage curves until the 12th cycle, but there is the decreasing charging voltage after 13th cycle, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S3. The capacity for 5.0 M reaches 220 mAh over 50 cycles (initial capacity of 270 mAh) indicating that the properties of the SZIRBs such as the voltage drop and stability are not simply attribute to the electrolyte resistance which increases with electrolyte concentration.

Cyclic voltammograms (CV) are acquired from the graphite felt (GF) in ZnI2 solutions in the potential range of −1.2–0.4 V vs. SCE as shown in Fig. 2 a (scanning rate of 100 mV-s−1). The electrolyte concentration change from 1 to 5 M corresponds to panel II to V and the CV curve of the graphite stick in 3 M ZnI2 is also shown in panel I for comparison. Similar CVs and oxidation-reduction behavior are observed in this potential region. They are typical oxidation-reduction curves for a carbon electrode in the aqueous ZnI2 solution [8]. Beyond the double electric layer region corresponding to the rectangular window (from −0.8 to 0 V), there are two pairs of redox reaction peaks corresponding to the redox Zn/Zn+ at the negative potential position when the potential is lower than about −1.0 V. When the potential sweep is reversed at −1.2 V, the anodic peak appears corresponding to the stripping process of deposited Zn metal. As for the positive potential, oxidation of 3I− to I3 − appears when the potential is bigger than 0.3 V. The reverse reduction process is shown in cathodic peak at about 0 V. Compared to the graphite stick, the CV curves show wide rectangular windows (large current density) for the graphite felt due to the large adsorption effect rendered by the three-dimensional porous structure.

For the graphite felt electrode, the current densities increases from 18 (10) to 28 (15) mA cm−2 in the CV curves with the increasing ZnI2 concentration from 1 to 2 M corresponding to the redox reaction of Zn/Zn2+ (I3 −/I−). Then the current densities of peaks decrease with the continual increasing concentration due to the large solution resistances. Such as, when the concentration reach 5 M, the CV curve shows a wide square structure and the current density of redox reactions of Zn/Zn2+ and I3 −/3I− decrease to ∼2 mA cm−2 due to the largest solution resistance (75 Ω). However the current density of redox reactions of Zn/Zn2+also decrease (∼18 mA cm−2) even the resistance is lowest for 3 M ZnI2 solution (3.1 Ω).

In the Zn–I redox battery, zinc ions are the redox active species and charge carriers and the zinc stripping/plating process influences the static Zn–I redox battery [8]. In order to understand the plating/stripping process of Zn, successive cyclic voltammograms (CV) are acquired from GF from a high potential −0.3 V to the low potential range between −0.7 and −1.1 V at a scanning rate 10 mV-s−1 for different Zn–I concentrations as shown in Fig. 2b–e. When the voltage envelope is extended from −0.75 to −0.95 V as shown in Fig. 2b, the CV curve of GF shows a square shape in the broad potential range in the 1.0 M ZnI2 solution. As the electrode potential changes to more negative values such as −1.0 V, the current density increases steadily due to zinc deposition at the first negative potential, a process termed overpotential deposition (OPD). The inverse potential scan shows a crossover of the cathodic and anodic branches typical of the formation of a new phase involving nucleation [28]. There is a new anodic peak at −0.9 V indicating dissolution of plated zinc. With the increasing of Zn–I concentration, new wide oxidation peaks emerge in the potential region of −0.5 V for the 2 and 3 M Zn–I solutions (Fig. 2c and d). There is also a new reduction peak at −0.8 V for the 3.0 M Zn–I electrolyte. When the concentration reaches 5 M (Fig. 2e), weak oxidation peaks appear in the potential range from −0.7 to −0.5 V.

To determine the origin of the oxidation peaks, successive cyclic voltammetry with stepwise increasing negative potential limits from −0.3 V to the potential range between −0.7 and −1.1 V is conducted at a scanning rate from 10 to 100 mV-s−1. The successive CVs of GF in the 3 M Zn–I solution are shown in Fig. 3 for the negative limit potentials up to (a) −0.7, (b) −0.75, (c) −0.8, and (d) −0.85. When the negative limit potential is more positive than - 0.75 V, the CVs are flat throughout the potential region from an initial potential - 0.3 V to a final potential - 0.7 V and non-oxidation peaks appear (Fig. 3a) characteristic of the charging/discharging process of the electric double-layer region. For the negative limit potential - 0.75V (Fig. 3b), the wide flat curves are observed for large sweeping rate. When the sweeping rate is small (10 - 20 mV-s−1), weak oxidation peaks appear at about - 0.52 V. Oxidation is related to the reduction process in the limited potential region (- 0.75 V) due to Zn deposition. The relationship between the oxidation/reduction peaks can also be identified when the limited negative potential approaches −0.8 V (Fig. 3c). When the reduction peaks appear at the potential of - 0.78 V, a series of oxidation peaks appear in the −0.5 V region. The first oxidation peak appears at −0.57 V, the second one at −0.50 V, and the third one at −0.45 V. The reduction and oxidation peaks are also sensitive to the sweeping rates. Clear oxidation and reduction peaks appear at low sweeping rates (10 - 30 mV-s−1) and as the sweeping rate goes up, the intensity of the oxidation peaks decreases and they overlap at high potentials. When the negative potential limit reaches −0.85 V (Fig. 3d), there are a clear reduction peak at −0.8 V and three oxidation peaks at −0.5 V. The reduction peak at the low potential of −0.8 V is suspected to be pre-deposition zinc oxidation [8,29–32]. The oxidation peaks can be ascribed to the striping process of zinc and related compounds as confirmed by EDS (to be discussed later).

To study the influence of the electrolyte concentration, CVs are obtained from GF as shown in Fig. 4 for different concentrations of Zn–I in the potential range between −0.3 and −1.05 V. The sweeping rate increases from 10 to 100 mV-s−1. For the 1.0 M Zn–I solution (Fig. 4a), the current density of the zinc stripping peak (−0.9 V) is 4 mA cm−2 for a sweeping rate 10 mV-s−1. With regard to larger sweeping rates, the Zn stripping peak decreases to 3 mA cm−2 and there are weak oxidation peaks at −0.45 V. For the 2.0 M Zn–I solution (Fig. 4b), there are increasing reduction peaks at −1.0 V with increasing sweeping rates and the current density reaches about 4 mA cm−2. When the potential is reversed, the current densities of the Zn stripping peak (−0.9 V) is about 6 mA-cm−2 for a sweeping rate of 10 mV-s−1 and decreases steadily with increasing sweeping rates. At the same time, the oxidation peaks at −0.45 V increase from 1 to 8 mA cm−2 indicating oxidized zinc deposited on the surface of graphite hinders the striping process of zinc and a larger potential is needed for stripping. With respect to the 3.0 M Zn–I solution (Fig. 4c), the reduction peak appears at −1.0 V and the current density is 8 mA cm−2 for a sweeping rate 100 mV-s−1. The stripping peak of plated Zinc decreases from 5 mA cm−2 with increasing sweeping rates. At the same time, the current densities of the series of oxidation peaks increase with sweeping rates and the current density reaches 11 mA cm−2 at −0.4 V for a sweeping rate of 100 mV-s−1. When the Zn–I concentration is increased to 5.0 M (Fig. 4d), the current density of the reduction peak at −1.0 V is only about 3 mA cm−2 for a sweeping rate of 100 mV- s−1 due to the large concentration resistance. Because of less stripping of plated zinc, the series of oxidation peaks can only reach 2 mA cm−2 at −0.45 V. That is, the maximum current intensity is observed from the 3 M Zn–I electrolyte. The oxidation peak areas versus Zn–I concentrations are shown in Fig. 4d for the negative potential limit reaching −1.05 V at different sweeping rates. The results show the maximum value when the concentration is 3 M and it is similar to the voltage drop observed from the battery.

In order to study the influence of iodine and oxidation on the plating process of zinc, SEM and EDS are performed to examine the morphology and determine the element concentrations. For the 3 M ZnI solution, different potentials are selected to deposit zinc for 20 min, including the low (−1.05 V) and high (−0.8 V) potentials. The results are presented in Fig. 5 . At a high potential, limited zinc is deposited on the graphite felt to form small zinc particles (Fig. 5a). EDS shows that almost the same ratio of iodine deposition on the graphite felt (2.1%) as well as deposition of a large oxidation species concentration on the graphite felt surface (7.7%) that is almost three times larger than those of zinc and iodide. Even at a low potential (Fig. 5b), a thick zinc film is deposited on the graphite felt and almost the same ratio of zinc and iodine are deposited (∼32%). A large ratio of oxidized species is also observed (∼17.6%) and it is almost the same ratio as that the carbon element (18.4%). The electrochemical data indicate that the plating process forms a zinc oxide layer on the graphite felt to hinder Zn stripping at the anode. The low discharge voltage and cycle stability of the ZINRB can be deduced due to the limited striping process of zinc, epically at a high concentration of ZnI2 such as 3 M. For the higher concentration such as 5 M, the large solution resistance (∼75 Ω) decreases diffusion of ions to hinder complexation between zinc and iodine on the anode. In this case, a high discharge voltage and better cycling stability are observed from the 5 M ZnI2 solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots coated graphite felt (GQD-GF)
ce:section.ce:para: According to the experimental data, it indicates that the electrolyte resistance and complexion of zinc and iodide on the surface of GF electrode would be the important parameters to influence the performance of SZIRBs. The later would be the first consideration at the large operation current. The deposition process of zinc oxide and complexation between Zn and iodine ions, for example formation of [ZnI3⋅5H2O]+ [8], occur in the charging/discharging process of the static redox Zn–I battery. It is possible to introduce species such as alcohol [8] to modify the interactions between zinc and triiodide and hinder the formation of zinc oxide and complexation. However, addition of alcohol to the Zn–I solution increases the electrolyte resistance. The oxidizing species on the electrode and/or in the solution influence the formation of zinc oxide and compromise the performance of the SZIRB. In order to modify the surface properties, graphene quantum dots are coated onto the graphite felts which have been applied widely to energy devices [33–38]. The properties of the synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQD) are described in Supporting Information. Owing to the small size of the graphene quantum dots, GQD-GF exhibits a similar and even smoother surface morphology as the pristine GF (Supplementary Fig. S5). The GQDs size of 5 nm can be identified by TEM (Supplementary Fig. S6a) together with a 0.36 nm lattice spacing of the graphene layers. The GQD solution shows UV absorption of GQDs at 362 nm and bright blue emission (fluorescence peak at 465 nm) under excitation by 365 nm UV light (Supplementary Fig. S6b). The clear peaks in the absorption (upper) and fluorescence (lower) spectra confirm formation of graphene quantum dots in the aqueous solution. XPS spectra shows the large ratio of oxidized species (Supplementary Fig. S7), for example, carbon/oxide ratio 4.2 and C–C/CC of only 46.5% for the pristine GF. By comparing the XPS data acquired from the GQD coated graphite felts, the carbon concentration increases and the oxidized species decrease after coating with GQDs. The ratio of carbon/oxidized specie increases to 8.9, and the ratio of C–C/CC increases to 63%. At the same time, the oxidized specie decreases, for example, the ratio of C–O (CO) decreasing from 19.3 (16.1) to 16.1% (13.2%) and the ratio of O–CO decreasing from 18.1 to 7.7%.

The cyclic voltammograms (CV) acquired from the GQD-graphite felt in ZnI2 solutions are shown in Supplementary Fig. S8 for the potential range of −1.2–0.5 V (sweeping rate of 100 mV-s−1). The electrolyte concentrations are changed from 1 to 5 M corresponding to panels I to III. Similar cyclic voltammograms and oxidation-reduction behavior are observed in this potential region. There are two pairs of redox reaction peaks corresponding to the redox Zn/Zn+ at the negative potential position when the potential is less than −1.0 V. When the potential sweep is reversed at −1.2 V, the anodic peak appears corresponding to the striping process of deposited Zn metal. For the positive potential, the oxidation process of 3I− to I3 − appears when the potential is higher than 0.3 V. The reverse reduction process is shown in the cathodic peak at about 0 V.

Fig. 6 a and b shows the successive cyclic voltammograms of GQD-GF in the 3 and 5 M ZnI2 solutions, respectively, acquired from a high potential of −0.1 V to the low potential range between −0.65 and −0.9 V at a scanning rate 10 mV-s−1. There is a low current density of the oxidation peak at the potential region from −0.4 to −0.2 V for the 3 and 5 M ZnI2 solutions in spite of the stripping peak of zinc at −0.8 V. The weak peaks are associated with less deposition of zinc oxide and related compounds because that the smaller oxide constituent on the graphite felt after coating with GQDs. The cyclic voltammograms (CV) of GF in 3 M concentration Zn–I aqueous solution is also shown in Fig. 6 from a high potential −0.1 V to limited negative potentials of −0.65, −0.7, −0.75, and −0.8 V. The sweeping rates are increased from 10 to 100 mV-s−1. When the negative limit potential reaches −0.65 V (Fig. 6c), the CVs are flat throughout the potential region from the initial potential of −0.1 V to a final potential −0.65 V and there are weak reduction and reverse oxidation processes. For the negative limit potentials of −0.7 and −0.75 V (Fig. 6d and e), there are only weak reduction peaks (−0.6 V) and oxidation peaks (−0.3 V). As for the negative limit potential of −0.85 V (Fig. 6f), there are only weak oxidation peaks (less than 1 mA cm−2) in the potential range from −0.4 to −0.2 V, indicating that the electrode limits the other reactions such as oxidation of zinc and complexation between iodide and zinc ions, corresponding to the decreasing oxidation peaks intensity due to surface modification rendered by the GQD coating process.

The charging-discharging curves of the GQD-GF electrodes (7 periods) are shown in Fig. 1 and the properties are presented in Table 1. For the low current case (100 mA), the battery capacity reaches 240 mAh and the capacity is stable throughout the 7 cycles for 3 M Zn–I. When the Zn–I concentration is increased to 5 M, the battery capacity is 220 mAh and increases to 240 mAh after 7 cycles. As shown in Table 1, the performance is relatively poor at a low current of 100 mA and high Zn–I concentration including the discharge capacity (energy) and Coulombic (voltage, energy) efficiency. However, the characteristics improve at a higher current of 200 mA. The capacity is 235 mAh and increases to 250 mAh after 7 cycles for 3 M Zn–I. When the Zn–I concentration goes up to 5 M, the capacity is 255 mAh and increases to 270 mAh after 7 cycles. The discharge capacity and Coulombic (voltage, energy) efficiency increase. The discharge energy increase from 254 to 273 mWh for 3 M Zn–I due to the increasing voltage efficiency from 74.4 to 82.8%, and with respect to 5 M Zn–I, the discharge capacity increases from 264 to 277.8 mAh, the discharge energy increases from 280 to 285 mWh, and the Coulombic efficiency increases from 85.2 to 89.6%.

The charging and discharging characteristics of the SZIRB with the GQD-GF electrode are listed in Table 1. At a low current of 100 mA, the charging and discharging potentials and voltagedrop are almost the same as that of the SZIRB in the 3 M ZnI2 solution. The voltagedrop increases from 0.09 V to 0.23 V for the SZIRB in the 5 M ZnI2 solution due to the increasing charging voltage (from 1.23 to 1.30 V) and decreasing discharging voltage (from 1.14 to 1.07 V). At a current of 200 mA, the voltagedrop decreases from 0.34 V to 0.22 V for the SZIRB in the 3 M ZnI2 solution due to the decreasing charging voltage (from 1.33 to 1.28 V) and increasing discharging voltage (from 0.99 to 1.06 V). Similarly, the voltagedrop decreases from 0.34 V to 0.23 V for the SZIRB in the 5 M ZnI2 solution. The charging and discharging voltages versus Zn–I concentrations are shown in Fig. 1c which shows the large voltage drop in the high concentration ZnI2 solution. At the large current, the decrease voltage drop and better properties of the ZRISB are related to the reversible Zn deposition/stripping process after the surface is modified with graphene quantum dots. In this case, the cycling number of the static redox Zn–I battery increases for a high current of 200 mA, as shown in Fig. 1d. The discharge capacity of the battery in the 3 M solution reaches 250 mAh and is stable for 45 cycles. The charging-discharging voltage curve is shown in Supplementary Fig. S3. When the Zn–I concentration goes up to 5 M, the discharge capacity is 270 mAh and remains the same for 40 cycles. The results confirm the good stability at large currents for the static Zn–I redox battery with the GQD-GF electrode.

Graphene quantum dots are introduced to graphite felt to fabricate a highly stable static zinc-iodine redox battery. The carbon concentration increases and the oxidized species decrease on the electrode surface. Oxidation of plated zinc and complexation between zinc and iodine on the anode are hindered in the solution with a high concentration of ZnI2. The static Zn–I redox battery constructed with the graphene quantum dot coating on graphite felts can be operated at high currents and show excellent energy density, Coulombic (voltage, energy) efficiency, and cycling stability. The results reveal the high performance and potential of the static Zn–I redox battery with the graphene quantum dots coating on graphite felt.

Yizhi Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Nan Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Zhe Wang: Investigation. Shaohui Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Dayuan Xiong: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lianwei Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Pingxiong Yang: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Paul K. Chu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61176108), Research Innovation Foundation of ECNU (No. 78210245), and City University of Hong Kong Strategic Research Grant (SRG No. 7005505).
